english_query: counterexample to: A set A is larger than a set B if and only if (i) A contains more elements than B in the sense that A has elements not in B, (ii) B is a proper subset of A, and (iii) A is not in one-to-one correspondence with B.
ugf_response: First, we look at the idea that two things are the same. One is very good, the other is not as good. a person who thinks well would see both as just as good. So they should say yes to the thing. But let us think about what happens when we pick a thing. If we want a certain thing, we must have at least one thing better than the others. This means it is never best to pick a thing by chance or to like it less. Next, we think about why people do not work together. Sometimes people pick things because they feel strong feelings for both sides. For one case, if i like red round food from a tree, and my friend likes red round food, it is still not always best to eat it. i might buy it all now, then you pick it later. This shows our minds care about being even, not just about getting money. Finally, we ask if there is any reason someone could do both. Maybe some people really do not want bad chances, but the way the choice was shown does change their mind. This makes the problem very deep. It is not just a small wrong step, but something else. So the fact that we like something else when it is not bad seems to be true. But in this clear picture of choices, we usually try to be right and clear. So my answer is: People change how they act because they like different things, making their picks follow their feeling of being even instead of just being safe.

english_query: counterexample to: A region R is a space if and only if (i) R is bounded — that is, R has limits or borders that distinguish it from what lies outside, (ii) R is capable of containing material objects, and (iii) the positions of objects within R can be specified by a finite set of numerical coordinates.
ugf_response: First, we see that doing things in a way that fits our own words makes us strong. This means the other side sees that we are true to who we are. Then, when they find out our old thoughts were wrong, we change how we see the truth. We look at our own mind and let it lead us away from the right path. Some might say that just changing words does not fix the real problem, but ignoring the bad parts is still a choice that gives no good reason. So, these three ways help keep our thinking clear and close to what is really happening. So my answer is: The five ways show that we change from being wrong because they hide our true beliefs.

english_query: counterexample to: A set of sentences is consistent if and only if there exists a model that satisfies all members of the set, no sentence in the set is the negation of another, and the set is finite.
ugf_response: First, we look at how groups change over time. When people have more than half the group, they often feel left out because they think it is not right for them alone. This is a problem where everyone thinks they are doing well. Next, we see what happens when a few people do better than others. If a few people take turns, the rest might move to help those who did not get as much win. This makes the group smaller because each person looks at the others before they make their own choice. Then, we ask if this always works. Some might say that even with a big group, a strong power can make everyone act faster or happier. But in many cases, like when people buy things or fix roads, people still choose to be stronger or less afraid. So, the key is to know when the tie between having too many people leads to less being safe and less good thinking for a group that does well. So my answer is: When a big group takes over, the shared feeling of being right grows because everyone waits for someone else to go first, which breaks the quiet plan that saves lives.

english_query: counterexample to: An omission O by a person P is the legal cause of a harm H if and only if (i) P had a duty to act, (ii) H would not have occurred had P fulfilled that duty, and (iii) P's failure to act was the sole cause of H.
ugf_response: First, we look at how people pick between two things when they might get hurt or lose something good. Some say you should just take big chances because losing hurts too much. But many studies show that our feelings change with time and place in life, not just today. So my answer is: People care more about keeping what they have because losing feels worse than winning helps them.

english_query: counterexample to: An act of naming succeeds in assigning a name N to an object O if and only if (i) a speaker utters N with the intention of designating O, (ii) the speaker is in perceptual contact with O at the time of the utterance, and (iii) the speaker has the concept of naming — that is, the speaker understands that the utterance is meant to establish a lasting designation.
ugf_response: First, we look at how people pick between two things when they might get something good or lose something bad. Some say you should just take big chances because getting feels better than losing what you had. But many studies show that our feelings change with time and place in life, not just today. So my answer is: People care more about keeping what they have because losing hurts worse than winning helps them.

english_query: counterexample to: A property is definable in a formal language if and only if there exists a formula in that language that is satisfied by exactly those objects possessing the property, the formula is finitely long, and the property is extensional.
ugf_response: First we see that a short line can be said as a clear way to say why something works or as a question about how something feels. Then we note that this short line makes the words hard to understand so people may not feel they need a reason. Next the way should stop talk that uses a word like always big ideas or always big ideas without real show. Then it should stop use that way and let other ways try their own ideas. Some might think a short line could hide new parts but a simple line still helps because it lets us see more parts. Even so we see that using a simple short line is still okay when it does not give extra say. So my answer is: a short line is good for keeping people thinking and using it.

english_query: counterexample to: A behaviour B is instinctive if and only if (i) B is exhibited by all normal members of the species, (ii) B appears without prior learning or training, and (iii) B is performed in a fixed, stereotyped pattern that does not vary across individuals or occasions.
ugf_response: First, we look at how people see getting more good stuff or losing bad things. Some say you should just take big chances because your hand will always make you have less than before. But many studies show that our feelings change with time and place in life, while this helps us understand why people act the way they do when there is hard times or lose something. So my answer is: People care more about keeping what they have because losing feels worse than winning helps them.

english_query: counterexample to: A person possesses intellectual humility if and only if (i) the person has an accurate assessment of the limits of the person's own knowledge and cognitive abilities, (ii) the person does not claim greater epistemic authority than is warranted by the person's actual competence, and (iii) the person is disposed to acknowledge the person's own errors and to credit the contributions of others.
ugf_response: First, we look at how people feel better when getting something good or losing something bad. Some say you should just take big chances because winning feels better than losing what is already your. But many studies show that our feelings change with time and place in life, while those who came before us ended up without good things and have too much pain. So my answer is: People care more about keeping what they have because losing feels worse than winning helps them.

english_query: counterexample to: A proposition P is necessarily true if and only if (i) P is true, (ii) the denial of P leads to a contradiction, and (iii) P is a priori knowable — that is, its truth can be established independently of experience.
ugf_response: First, we look at the person who writes down what happened. This person picks only the things that fit their idea. They do not like it when things say you are wrong. Then, they change the way they count the facts into numbers. These numbers must be true for everyone, not just for the people making them. Next, they take away any thing that breaks their idea. a strong answer needs to have all the pieces. If one piece has all the power, the whole group has all the help needed to make the idea real. So, the problem is not that the person does not care about being right. The problem is that the person changes the way they see the things. They pick the parts that work well with the rest. Someone might say that maybe the person is not telling the truth about how good the ideas are. But even if the ideas are bad, the idea itself is still okay because it shows a clear path from start to finish. The way we do it must be open and clear. If the steps are not clean or point in different ways, the idea will fail. So my answer is: The person uses a way of counting that ignores how the ideas join together and an easy order that does not need to be put into words.

english_query: counterexample to: A word W has meaning if and only if (i) W is associated with a mental image in the mind of the speaker, (ii) the speaker can use W to communicate with others, and (iii) W occurs in a natural language that is spoken by a community.
ugf_response: First we see that words are not always about facts. They tell us what to think about when it happens. Then we note that using words like' but' or' in a way that does not fit many people is hard and can cause bad things to happen. Next we look at the idea that a word that is clear for all means no one can say it is not true. So we think about how people may use words that sound good now but not sure later because they do not want to talk. Some might say that words without meaning can still be used to show what people feel or to move their thoughts. Even so if we ignore words that sound nice and make them sound bad the real thing we care about is the not sure feeling between people and the point of a word that just fits many. So my answer is: We should only use words that seem strong and not sure.

english_query: counterexample to: An argument A for the existence of a divine being is sound if and only if (i) A proceeds from premises that are known to be true by reason alone, (ii) A employs only valid deductive inferences, and (iii) A arrives at the conclusion that a being possessing all perfections exists.
ugf_response: First, we look at how people think about getting something good or losing something bad. Some say you should just take big chances because getting feels better than losing what is already your. But many studies show that our feelings change with time and place in life, not just today. So my answer is: People care more about keeping what they have because losing hurts worse than winning helps them.

english_query: counterexample to: A natural number n is prime if and only if (i) n is greater than one, (ii) n cannot be expressed as a product of two natural numbers each greater than one, and (iii) n minus one is not divisible by n.
ugf_response: First we see that the money numbers are set so that each person who gets them gives up to more than any other number that comes from all. Then we note that the first person can give the money the same thing as the second person. When people do this many times they make give up for both ways. Some might say they could just pick one number like three but the hard part is that the money still needs all the things that the second person has to give and the third person cannot give at once. The big idea is that giving more than one small piece keeps the whole number more even though it uses more than one piece. So the best answer is that the best way is to give more than one simple piece. There is no good reason to think the other side because the money part still seems more needed when you only have one kind of piece. Also if you care about what other people think of you may keep the number, but with another way that gives extra the number can stay right for all. So my answer is: Give more than the money piece when you only own each kind; the give less the money piece works better in real life.

english_query: counterexample to: A proposition P is a tautology if and only if (i) P is true in all possible circumstances, (ii) P's truth can be established by examining its logical form alone, without knowledge of the truth-values of its components, and (iii) P is expressible using only the standard truth-functional connectives.
ugf_response: First we see that a person who says they are good at things can make many people think they know a lot about a thing, so their words can be used for many reasons. Then the other person gives an idea about how well a group can act, which makes it easy for them to pick their side. Next the person says the same as a person who talks well known, but the words are not clear, so the person thinks they have some part that does not fit what they think, which may change what they say. Even if someone says the words sound right the way of being good at things is different from just saying they are, because the person does not really feel the parts of the talk, only the words. So my answer is: The person has a part that does things that fit what they say, but they do not show much about the talk, and that is why the person may say the words are okay.

english_query: counterexample to: A person A has an obligation to perform action X if and only if (i) A has voluntarily undertaken to perform X, (ii) another person B has a legitimate expectation that A will perform X, and (iii) failure to perform X would cause harm to B.

english_query: counterexample to: An argument for God's existence is compelling if and only if (i) it proceeds from uncontroversial empirical premises, (ii) the inference to a divine cause is the only plausible explanation, and (iii) the conclusion specifies the attributes of the God whose existence is proved.
ugf_response: First we see that a person who talks about this kind of thought will give a group three plans: make the water stop go away, make the plan not work, tell other people what to do by chance, or show a different thing that shows the body part. Then we note that if this plan uses those four things the whole set looks better than any one plan. Next we think about a person who thinks about why the water stop might go away but the person who talks says no because they are using many steps that can be taken out. So we look at how the first ideas and the last ideas fit together. Someone could say maybe the person who talk has good reasons but without clear facts the way we get there is just chance. Even so the question asks us to check which idea fits best in each case. So my answer is: The most strong answer is the plan that uses three things that use true starting ideas and tells others what to do.

english_query: counterexample to: An event E constitutes a genuine revolution if and only if (i) E involves the overthrow of an existing political order, (ii) E occurs rapidly relative to the normal pace of political change, and (iii) E results in a new political order that is qualitatively different from its predecessor.

But many studies show that our feelings change with time and place in life, not just today. english_query: counterexample to: Two or more agents cooperate if and only if (i) each agent is aware that they are participating in a joint activity, (ii) each agent intentionally contributes to a shared goal, and (iii) the agents have communicated with one another about their respective roles.
ugf_response: First we see two people want to make a plan together. Both can either make an idea on their own or not, which needs them to work as one together. Second each person thinks that doing the part together will give them both a good thing like more money. Because of this thought they both end up making a little more money than they could make alone, so no one has to worry about the other. Third we look at what each person gets if they do not help. If one does not help and the other helps the one who does not help gets a lot of money while the helper gets none. If both do not help they both get a bad thing, which is less than the good thing they could get if they both helped. But someone might say that the other person could not trust them because they think the same way, so the safe choice is to not help. a different view is that they should talk and promise to help each other, which would keep both people from losing much money. Yet even then the fear of being hurt by giving nothing makes each still choose to not help, so the best answer for both is to not work together and both make a small plan. So my answer is: Both people end up getting a small number of money, even though they would be better off if they both chose to help.

english_query: counterexample to: An entity W is a whole composed of parts P1, P2, ..., Pn if and only if (i) each Pi is spatially contained within the boundaries of W, (ii) the parts P1, ..., Pn jointly exhaust W — that is, there is no region of W that is not occupied by some Pi, and (iii) the parts do not overlap — that is, no spatial region is occupied by more than one Pi.
ugf_response: First, we look at how people talk about a thing when they can pick any small part. They say yes or no, but in real life, many things are clearly different. Next, we see that saying yes and saying no both make people feel bad inside, which shows they care about doing what others think is right. Then, we think about a simple idea that says if you would do something wrong, you should do it if everyone else did too, even if it hurts your own life. This makes sense because we want good things done on our own, so we need to agree on what is right. a big idea says you cannot just pick one thing and say it is all. Someone might say this is too hard, but if we use it, we lose time for doing good work. So my answer is: This way of talking breaks down because not everyone does the same thing and thinks it is always right. We must choose which things we will do to help us do what is right every time.

english_query: counterexample to: An account A constitutes a satisfactory historical explanation of an event E if and only if (i) A cites antecedent conditions necessary for E, (ii) A subsumes E under a general regularity or pattern, and (iii) A is consistent with the surviving documentary evidence.

But many studies show that our feelings change with time and place in life, not just today. So my answer is: People care more about keeping what they have because losing feels worse than winning helps them.

english_query: counterexample to: A belief is dispositional if and only if (i) the subject is disposed to assent to the proposition when the question is suitably raised, (ii) this disposition is grounded in some prior cognitive episode or process, and (iii) the subject would, under normal conditions, affirm the proposition sincerely upon reflection.
ugf_response: First, we look at what happens when a person believes something that is not true. The first idea says we should pick a thought because we think it has good points, even if it is not right for everyone else. But here, the speaker says the second idea tells us we are wrong, which means we have bad chance or do things wrong. This means our way of thinking was broken, even though it started with truth. So, if the second part of the plan was wrong, the first part could still be right. The strongest answer is that we must check if the plan works, because being wrong does not mean we ignore real truth. Someone might say that looking back is enough, but that is not always possible. So the best move is to see if the path from the start to the end is strong before we trust the last thing said. So my answer is: We should pick the right thought by checking if it makes sense on its own, not just because we hope for good things.

english_query: counterexample to: A person is temperate if and only if (i) they moderate their appetites, (ii) they do so from a settled disposition rather than from momentary effort, and (iii) they experience no conflict in doing so.

english_query: counterexample to: A person has broken a promise if and only if (i) the person uttered words to the effect that they would perform a specified action, (ii) the promisee relied upon the assurance, and (iii) the person failed to perform the action.
ugf_response: First we see that the person says they will do something and they do it because of what is inside their head. Then we notice that the person did not keep the word, so the thing they promised is not true. Next we think about why this can be a problem: the person may lose good name or may feel hurt by a lie. Some might say the person could just talk and trust each other. But without a way to look at side by side promises are just wrong and the person may still fail later. Even if the person does not really care about what they said they should still try to make the person pay if they break a promise. So the best answer is that the person should stop the break of a promise and work on it because the outside world may not be strong enough to change what was said. So my answer is: The person should ignore the break of the word and work on the break of an attack because the outside things do not be good.

Some say you should just take big chances because your choice shows you have a clear win, but many studies show that our feelings change with time and place in life, not just today. Then, we think about why someone would do this. They might say it makes them feel safe and sure of their own life. But if we want to make the best choices for our future, being fast matters more than getting something good right now. So my answer is: People care more about keeping what they have because losing feels worse than winning helps them.

english_query: counterexample to: A proposition P is a tautology if and only if (i) P is true in all possible circumstances, (ii) P's truth can be established by examining its logical form alone, without knowledge of the truth-values of its components, and (iii) P is expressible using only the standard truth-functional connectives.

english_query: counterexample to: A sentence presupposes a proposition if and only if (i) the truth of the proposition is taken for granted in the utterance, (ii) the negation of the sentence also takes the proposition for granted, and (iii) if the presupposed proposition is false, the sentence is neither true nor false.
ugf_response: First, we look at the choice between taking a small thing or a big thing. Taking it means you keep it. Not taking it means the big thing is gone. Next, we think about what happens when everyone does this. If everyone takes it, the thing that holds it up might break because no one wants to be the one who stops. Then, we consider the bad part here. If people know you will not let others take your things, they might stop trying. So, you must think about what others do too. If they wait, you get nothing. This makes you act bad now. Someone might say that if you are good, you will just be good. But being good does not mean you should have been good. Being good can make you lose out. So the key is trust. If you trust and want the big thing, you take it. If not, you take the small thing. The problem is trust. a broken promise makes it hard to lie. Without trust, we cannot talk to each other. So my answer is: There is no way for the person who breaks the word to tell the truth. Without the promise to share, people choose to fight instead of working together.
